message:
chore(beads): close 5 already-shipped REVIEW beads via JSONL

DB write path is wedged behind two stuck `br sync` processes; closing
through JSONL edit per workflow fallback. All five beads have their
implementation already shipped and verified:

- bd-z4k96 (HIGH locale unbounded reads): all 4 named sites
  (setlocale, textdomain, bindtextdomain, newlocale) use the new
  read_bounded_cstr helper. 50/50 locale_abi tests pass.
- bd-jqy64 (HIGH c11threads create/join mismatch): commit 138c07c3
  delegates pthread_join to host for non-managed handles. 37/37
  c11threads_abi_test tests pass.
- bd-9chy.7 (P2 stdio tmpfile native): O_TMPFILE primary +
  mkstemp/unlink fallback + fdopen_native_impl already in place;
  6/6 stdio tmpfile tests pass.
- bd-cie59 (HIGH cond_wait owner check): shipped in commit 64fb99b2
  with regression test condvar_wait_errorcheck_without_lock_returns_eperm.
- bd-ily44 (HIGH iconv UAF TOCTOU): shipped in commit c8003957 (peer)
  via Arc<Mutex<IconvDescriptor>> registry + concurrent-close
  regression test.

No code changes; JSONL-only.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-4rdz8","title":"REVIEW: [HIGH] aio_suspend panics on adversarial timespec (negative tv_sec / out-of-range tv_nsec) instead of returning EINVAL","description":"Location: crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/unistd_abi.rs:7934-7940 (aio_suspend), and by delegation aio_suspend64:15597.

Current code:
    let ts = unsafe { *timeout };
    let dur = std::time::Duration::new(ts.tv_sec as u64, ts.tv_nsec as u32);
    Some(std::time::Instant::now() + dur)

Defect:
1. `ts.tv_sec as u64` reinterprets a negative i64 (e.g. tv_sec = -1) as u64::MAX, producing a near-infinite Duration.
2. `ts.tv_nsec as u32` truncates: a negative tv_nsec or a value >= 2**32 is silently accepted.
3. `Instant::now() + dur` for a near-u64::MAX Duration panics with 'overflow when adding duration to instant', aborting the process.

POSIX contract: aio_suspend shall fail with EINVAL if timeout->tv_nsec < 0 or >= 1_000_000_000. A negative tv_sec is also invalid.

Severity: HIGH. Reachable from any C program calling aio_suspend with attacker-controlled (or merely corrupted) timespec — this is in the exported libc.so surface. An adversarial aio_suspend(NULL-list-with-nent=0 already rejected, else valid-list, &{.tv_sec=-1}) aborts the whole process.

Fix plan:
- Validate tv_sec >= 0 and tv_nsec in [0, 999_999_999]; return -1 with EINVAL otherwise.
- Use Instant::checked_add and fall back to 'no deadline' on overflow (which then polls every 100ms until completion, matching the no-timeout path).
- Add a regression unit test in the abi crate that passes an out-of-range timespec and asserts -1/EINVAL instead of panic.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T23:41:47.617220787Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-23T23:44:10.501229724Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T23:44:10.499013410Z","close_reason":"Fixed: crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/unistd_abi.rs aio_suspend now validates tv_sec >= 0 and tv_nsec in [0, 999_999_999], returning -1/EINVAL on violation, and uses Instant::checked_add to avoid the Duration-add panic on very large (but technically valid) tv_sec. Four regression tests added in crates/frankenlibc-abi/tests/unistd_abi_test.rs (aio_suspend_rejects_negative_tv_sec_without_panic / _negative_tv_nsec / _oversize_tv_nsec / _empty_list_before_timeout). Run: rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/rch_target_libc_review cargo test -p frankenlibc-abi --test unistd_abi_test aio_suspend -> 4/0 passed in 0.00s. cargo check --all-targets clean. aio_suspend64 inherits the fix via its unsafe { aio_suspend(...) } delegation at line 15597.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"bd-4rjgy","title":"[MODE-5][HIGH] symbolic correctness: runtime_policy env lock poisoning hides root failures","description":"[MODE-5][HIGH] symbolic correctness: runtime_policy tests collapse into lock poisoning after the first assertion failure

Fresh remote validation on the current tree:
- `env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/rch_target_frankenlibc_cod rch exec -- cargo test --workspace --all-targets`
- current failing crate path: `frankenlibc-abi::runtime_policy::tests`

Observed failures:
- `mode_logging_captures_startup_selection_and_switch_attempt` fails its primary assertion at `crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/runtime_policy.rs:1865-1867` because the exported log does not contain `runtime_mode_switch_attempt`.
- after that failure, three more tests panic at `crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/runtime_policy.rs:1699-1703` because `env_lock()` uses `.expect("env lock should not be poisoned")` on a shared `Mutex<()>`.

This turns one behavioral failure into four red tests and destroys fault isolation inside the suite.

Fix direction:
- fix the missing mode-switch logging behavior in `mode_logging_captures_startup_selection_and_switch_attempt`
- make `env_lock()` recover from poisoning in tests, for example with `unwrap_or_else(|e| e.into_inner())`, so one failure does not mask the rest of the signal.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"ubuntu","created_at":"2026-04-22T08:48:11.314520118Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-24T00:22:53.044490370Z","closed_at":"2026-04-24T00:22:53.042396632Z","close_reason":"Added runtime_policy env-lock unwind regression coverage; verified mode switch logging and ABI tests via rch","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["modes","review","runtime-policy","symbolic"]}

 {"id":"bd-9chy.47","title":"test: fopen/fdopen comprehensive unit + integration suite (mode parsing, isatty, errno, fd lifecycle, EINTR)","description":"## Background

bd-9chy.5 lists only one unit test in its done criteria. This bead is the comprehensive surface.

## Depends on

bd-9chy.5

## Test list

Mode parsing:
1. fopen("/tmp/x", "r") → O_RDONLY
2. fopen("/tmp/x", "w") → O_WRONLY|O_CREAT|O_TRUNC, mode 0666
3. fopen("/tmp/x", "a") → O_WRONLY|O_CREAT|O_APPEND
4. fopen("/tmp/x", "r+") → O_RDWR
5. fopen("/tmp/x", "w+") → O_RDWR|O_CREAT|O_TRUNC
6. fopen("/tmp/x", "a+") → O_RDWR|O_CREAT|O_APPEND
7. Each of the above with trailing "b" (binary, no-op on Linux)
8. Each of the above with trailing "x" (O_EXCL — fail if exists)
9. Trailing "e" (O_CLOEXEC) honored
10. Bad mode strings → NULL + EINVAL: "", "z", "rw", "ar", "rwe"

Path handling:
11. Long path (PATH_MAX) accepted
12. Path with spaces accepted
13. Path with embedded null → EINVAL
14. NULL path → EFAULT (or segfault — POSIX is permissive; document choice)

isatty buffering:
15. fopen("/dev/tty", "w") on a tty → buffer mode = _IOLBF
16. fopen("/dev/null", "w") → buffer mode = _IOFBF
17. fopen("/tmp/x", "w") on a regular file → _IOFBF

errno preservation:
18. Failed fopen sets errno appropriately (ENOENT, EACCES, EISDIR)
19. Successful fopen does NOT touch errno

Fd lifecycle:
20. Returned NativeFile fd is a fresh fd (not 0/1/2)
21. fclose closes the fd (verify with fstat returning EBADF after)
22. fclose called twice → second call fails with EINVAL (or detects double-close — POMDP healing in hardened mode)

fdopen:
23. fdopen of a fresh socket fd → NativeFile that supports read/write
24. fdopen of -1 → NULL + EBADF
25. fdopen with mode mismatching the fd open flags → EINVAL

EINTR:
26. fopen on a slow filesystem (LD_PRELOAD a slow openat) returning EINTR → retried transparently

## Done

- All 26 tests in tests/integration/fopen_fdopen_test.rs
- Each runs under both strict and hardened modes
- Tests use the real .so via LD_PRELOAD","notes":"Comprehensive fopen/fdopen tests exist in stdio_abi_test.rs. Unit tests requiring NativeFile streams are marked as needing LD_PRELOAD integration. 98 tests pass, 25 appropriately ignored for vtable validation constraints.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-09T00:40:26.393957331Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-13T19:03:03.390402718Z","closed_at":"2026-04-13T19:03:03.389785779Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["constructor","stdio","test"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-9chy.47","depends_on_id":"bd-9chy.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-09T00:40:26.393957331Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-9chy.5","title":"stdio: rewrite fopen/fopen64/fdopen to be native-only, returning *mut NativeFile","description":"## Background

The audit pinpoints these as the only unconditional host delegators in stdio_abi.rs. fopen at L676-729 calls host_fopen() under prefer_host_stdio_streams() and otherwise falls back to native openat + registry insert. fdopen at L4060-4108 same pattern. Every other stdio function downstream "checks registry first; delegates to host if alien" — meaning the *only* way alien pointers enter the system is through these two constructors. Eliminate the alien-pointer source and the rest of the cleanup follows naturally.

## Depends on

bd-9chy.1 (NativeFile must be _IO_FILE_plus-shaped before fopen/fdopen can return it without breaking existing pointer-arith assumptions).

## What to do

1. In crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/stdio_abi.rs, rewrite fopen() (L676-729):
   - Parse mode string into open flags (existing helper).
   - Call native openat raw syscall (already at L703-709 in the fallback branch — promote to primary).
   - Allocate a NativeFile from the slab allocator (or via the registry constructor — verify which exists).
   - Initialize: fd, vtable=&NATIVE_IO_JUMP_T, generation=fresh, mbstate=initial, orientation=Undecided, lock_count=0, open_flags=oflags, fingerprint=fresh siphash.
   - Set buffering mode based on isatty() raw syscall (NOT libc::isatty): _IOLBF if isatty, _IOFBF otherwise.
   - Insert into stream registry.
   - Return as *mut FILE (cast). NEVER call host_fopen.
2. fopen64 is an alias to fopen on 64-bit platforms — verify it stays that way and routes to the native path.
3. fdopen at L4060-4108 same surgery: take the input fd, build a NativeFile shell, set buffering mode based on isatty(fd), insert into registry, return.
4. Remove the prefer_host_stdio_streams() check from both functions. The function itself stays for now (deleted in a separate cleanup bead) but these two callers must not consult it.
5. Confirm the existing native-fallback branch in fopen (L694-729) becomes the primary and only branch.

## Cross-cutting

- The slab allocator for NativeFile should be a fixed-size pool to keep alloc/free deterministic. Coordinate with the malloc crate maintainer.
- isatty must be implemented as a raw ioctl(TCGETS) or fcntl probe — using libc::isatty would re-introduce host delegation.

## Done

- fopen/fopen64/fdopen do not call host_*_fn() under any branch
- A unit test calls fopen("/dev/null", "w"), inspects the returned pointer, confirms its in the registry, and verifies the vtable pointer is set
- Existing fopen tests still pass
- The fopen body has zero references to prefer_host_stdio_streams or host_fopen_fn","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-09T00:27:26.044561856Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-13T17:42:20.571271340Z","closed_at":"2026-04-13T17:42:20.570983165Z","close_reason":"Rewrote fopen/fopen64/fdopen to use native NativeFile path. No host delegation. Returns real *mut NativeFile pointers. Uses raw syscalls for isatty check. Registers in native_stream_registry.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["constructor","stdio"]}
 {"id":"bd-9chy.6","title":"stdio: implement popen/pclose natively via posix_spawn + native fdopen","description":"## Background

popen is FILE-acquisition channel #3 in plan v4 §1.10. Today it lives in process_abi.rs or stdio_abi.rs (verify) and almost certainly delegates to host libc since host pipe+fork+fdopen is non-trivial to replicate. After the fopen/fdopen bead lands (bd-9chy.5), popen must build a native NativeFile around the pipe end.

## Depends on

bd-9chy.1 (layout), bd-9chy.5 (native fdopen exists)

## What to do

1. Locate the current popen implementation. Likely in crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/process_abi.rs.
2. Replace with: native pipe2 raw syscall, native posix_spawn raw syscall (or fork+execve fallback for kernels without posix_spawn), close the unused pipe end in the parent, pass mode "r" or "w" to native fdopen on the surviving end.
3. pclose: read fd from the NativeFile, close it via native fclose path, then waitpid on the recorded child pid, return the child status. Store the child pid in a NativeFile extension field (add a popen_child_pid: Option<i32> to NativeFileState).
4. Both "r" and "w" modes must work; "re" (close-on-exec) must be honored.
5. An integration test runs `popen("echo hello", "r")`, fgets a line, pclose, asserts return == 0 and the line == "hello\n".

## Edge cases

- SIGCHLD handling: do not install a default SIG_IGN that would orphan zombies; let the user decide.
- popen inside a multi-threaded program: pthread_atfork handlers (separate bead bd-9chy-22 in the locking tier) must run before fork.
- Failure modes: spawn failure must close both pipe ends and return NULL with errno set.

## Done

- popen/pclose are native; no host delegation
- Both modes work; "re" honored
- Integration test passes
- popen_child_pid field added to NativeFileState","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-09T00:27:38.977289670Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-13T18:50:03.705447591Z","closed_at":"2026-04-13T18:50:03.704781310Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["constructor","stdio"]}
-{"id":"bd-9chy.7","title":"stdio: implement tmpfile/tmpfile64 natively via O_TMPFILE + native fdopen","description":"## Background

Channel #4 in plan v4 §1.10. POSIX tmpfile creates an unnamed temporary file that is auto-deleted on close. Linux >= 3.11 provides O_TMPFILE for atomic creation of an unnamed file in a directory; older kernels need mkstemp + unlink.

## Depends on

bd-9chy.1, bd-9chy.5

## What to do

1. Locate current tmpfile in stdio_abi.rs (verify).
2. Implement: try openat(AT_FDCWD, "/tmp", O_TMPFILE|O_RDWR, 0600). If succeeded, build a NativeFile around the fd via native fdopen path.
3. Fallback for older kernels: use a fresh mkstemp template (e.g. /tmp/franken-tmp-XXXXXX), open O_RDWR|O_CREAT|O_EXCL, then immediately unlink the path while keeping the fd open. Build NativeFile around it.
4. tmpfile64 is the alias on 64-bit platforms; verify it routes to the same code.
5. Test: tmpfile, fwrite "hello", fseek SEEK_SET, fread back, assert "hello", fclose. Verify the file is gone after fclose by trying to readdir /tmp for any leftover.

## Done

- O_TMPFILE primary path; mkstemp+unlink fallback
- No libc::tmpfile or host_*_fn references
- Integration test passes including the no-leftover check","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-09T00:27:46.987767309Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-24T01:51:38.679953443Z","closed_at":"2026-04-24T01:51:38.677996859Z","close_reason":"Verified tmpfile/tmpfile64 native O_TMPFILE plus mkstemp-unlink path with tmpfile64 alias readback and unlinked-open regression.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["constructor","stdio"]}

 {"id":"bd-9chy.8","title":"stdio: complete native fmemopen/open_memstream/open_wmemstream with NativeFile memory backing","description":"## Background

Channels #5/#6/#7 in plan v4 §1.10. The audit suggests these may be partially native already in crates/frankenlibc-core/src/stdio/file.rs (which has mem_backing in StdioStream). This bead is the cleanup-and-completion pass.

## Depends on

bd-9chy.1, bd-9chy.5

## What to do

1. Add a NativeFileBacking enum to NativeFileState: Fd(i32), MemoryFixed { buf: *mut u8, size: usize, owns: bool }, MemoryGrowing { buf: *mut *mut u8, size: *mut usize, capacity: usize }, Cookie { ... }.
2. fmemopen(buf, size, mode):
   - mode parsing identical to fopen
   - if buf is NULL, allocate a fresh size-byte buffer (owns=true); else use the caller buffer (owns=false)
   - the NativeFile carries Backing::MemoryFixed
   - reads return bytes from the buffer up to size; writes append until size, then return short with EOF set
   - "a" mode positions at first NUL or at size, whichever first
3. open_memstream(bufp, sizep):
   - allocate an initial 4 KiB growing buffer
   - on every fwrite that overflows, realloc to 2x capacity
   - on fclose or fflush, write the current buffer pointer and size to the user-provided bufp/sizep
   - the buffer must remain valid after fclose; only the NativeFile is freed
4. open_wmemstream(bufp, sizep): same as open_memstream but the orientation field is set to Wide at construction and locked.

 {"id":"bd-9dq5","title":"Track D: Implement gai_cancel/gai_error/gai_suspend as synchronous-only stubs with proper error returns","description":"## Goal
Replace 3 async DNS stub functions in unistd_abi.rs (lines 17404-17423) that incorrectly return EAI_SYSTEM/ENOSYS with correct POSIX error returns.

## Critical Design Insight
FrankenLibC does NOT export getaddrinfo_a() — the function that creates async DNS requests. Therefore, gai_error/gai_cancel/gai_suspend will NEVER receive valid in-flight request handles. The correct implementation is NOT a thread pool — it is simply returning the correct error code for 'no such request' or 'no pending requests'.

## Current State (WRONG)
All 3 functions set errno=ENOSYS and return EAI_SYSTEM. This is incorrect because:
- gai_cancel should return EAI_NOCANCEL (not a valid request) or EAI_ALLDONE
- gai_error should return EAI_SYSTEM with errno=EINVAL (invalid gaicb)
- gai_suspend with an empty/null list should return EAI_ALLDONE (no pending requests)

Programs that probe async DNS availability by calling gai_suspend(NULL, 0, NULL) get EAI_SYSTEM instead of EAI_ALLDONE, which may cause them to disable async features unnecessarily.

## Correct Implementation (SIMPLE)
### gai_cancel(req)
- If req is NULL: return EAI_NOCANCEL
- Otherwise: return EAI_ALLDONE (no async requests exist, so it is already 'done')

### gai_error(req)
- If req is NULL: set errno=EINVAL, return EAI_SYSTEM
- Otherwise: return 0 (treat any gaicb as 'completed' since we resolve synchronously)

### gai_suspend(list, nitems, timeout)
- If list is NULL or nitems <= 0: return 0 (nothing to wait for)
- Scan list entries: if all non-NULL entries have ar_result set, return 0
- If timeout is NULL: return EAI_AGAIN (would block forever waiting for nothing)
- If timeout is not NULL: return EAI_AGAIN (timeout immediately since no async work pending)

## Why This Is Correct
The POSIX spec says gai_suspend waits for 'at least one request to complete.' Since FrankenLibC resolves all getaddrinfo calls synchronously, any gaicb passed to gai_suspend has ALREADY been resolved by the time gai_suspend is called. The correct behavior is to check if the requests are done and return immediately.

## Testing
- Unit test: gai_cancel(NULL) returns EAI_NOCANCEL
- Unit test: gai_cancel(non_null_ptr) returns EAI_ALLDONE
- Unit test: gai_error(NULL) returns EAI_SYSTEM with errno=EINVAL
- Unit test: gai_suspend(NULL, 0, NULL) returns 0
- Unit test: gai_suspend(empty_list, 0, NULL) returns 0
- e2e: scripts/check_gai_async_stubs.sh verifying correct return codes via small C test

## Structured Logging
All 3 functions must emit structured trace on invocation:
  trace_id, mode, api_family=resolv, symbol=gai_cancel|gai_error|gai_suspend, decision_path, errno, latency_ns

## Acceptance Criteria
All 3 functions return correct POSIX error codes. No ENOSYS returns. Tests pass. Clippy clean. e2e script passes.

## Note
If getaddrinfo_a() is ever implemented in the future, these functions would need to be upgraded to poll a real request registry. But that is a separate epic (P4+) since async DNS is extremely rare.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-03T00:19:24.489961531Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-03T02:46:07.802361147Z","closed_at":"2026-04-03T02:46:07.802082449Z","close_reason":"Replaced incorrect ENOSYS returns with correct POSIX error codes. gai_cancel: NULL->EAI_NOTCANCELED, non-null->EAI_ALLDONE (all sync, nothing to cancel). gai_error: NULL->EAI_SYSTEM/EINVAL, non-null->0 (synchronous resolver means complete). gai_suspend: nitems<=0->0, otherwise->0 (all requests already resolved synchronously). No thread pool needed since getaddrinfo_a is not exported. Clippy clean.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["async","resolv","stub-elimination"],"comments":[{"id":1364,"issue_id":"bd-9dq5","author":"ubuntu","text":"Risk: gai_* async DNS used by almost no programs. ENOSYS acceptable for 99%+ workloads. If too complex, fallback: implement gai_error as immediate-return (EAI_ALLDONE for completed, EAI_NOCANCEL for unknown) and gai_suspend as no-wait poll. This synchronous stub passes most programs checking async DNS availability.","created_at":"2026-04-03T00:21:02Z"}]}

+{"id":"bd-cie59","title":"REVIEW: [HIGH] pthread_cond_wait/timedwait clobbers RECURSIVE/ERRORCHECK mutex owner without ownership check","description":"In crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/pthread_abi.rs lines 2541-2554 (pthread_cond_wait) and 2775-2788 (pthread_cond_timedwait), for PTHREAD_MUTEX_RECURSIVE_TYPE and PTHREAD_MUTEX_ERRORCHECK_TYPE the code unconditionally clears the mutex owner (owner.store(MUTEX_NO_OWNER)) and zeros the recursive lock count (count.swap(0)), without first verifying that the calling thread is actually the owner.

If thread A holds a recursive mutex with count=N and thread B incorrectly invokes pthread_cond_wait on that same mutex (a POSIX violation, but glibc detects it for ERRORCHECK), the current code:
1. Saves count (which belongs to A) into thread B's stack-local saved_count
2. Stores NO_OWNER, destroying A's ownership record
3. Resets count to 0, destroying A's recursion depth
4. Waits on the condvar
5. On wake, claims ownership for thread B and restores A's saved count to thread B

This effectively allows thread B to silently steal the mutex. POSIX mandates EPERM for pthread_cond_wait with an unlocked or non-owned ERRORCHECK mutex.

Repro: Two threads, mutex_init with PTHREAD_MUTEX_ERRORCHECK; thread A locks; thread B calls pthread_cond_wait without locking; expect EPERM, observe owner state corruption.

Fix: Before saving/clearing owner, read current owner via owner.load(Acquire) and compare to core_self_tid(). If mismatch and type is ERRORCHECK, return EPERM. For RECURSIVE, also return EPERM (POSIX says behavior is undefined, but EPERM is safer than corruption).","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-24T01:52:46.881702695Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-24T03:30:00Z","closed_at":"2026-04-24T03:30:00Z","close_reason":"Fixed: commit 64fb99b2 added owner verification in pthread_cond_wait/timedwait for RECURSIVE/ERRORCHECK; returns EPERM for non-owner. Regression test added.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

-{"id":"bd-ily44","title":"REVIEW: [HIGH] iconv() TOCTOU use-after-free with concurrent iconv_close()","description":"In crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/iconv_abi.rs lines 176-188, the iconv() function validates the descriptor handle via is_known_handle(cd) (HashSet lookup), then dereferences via 'unsafe { &mut *cd.cast::<IconvDescriptor>() }'. There is no synchronization between the lookup and the dereference.

If thread B calls iconv_close(cd) between thread A's is_known_handle check and the &mut * dereference, the descriptor's Box is freed (line 350: 'unsafe { Box::from_raw(cd.cast::<IconvDescriptor>()) }'), and thread A then dereferences freed memory --- use-after-free.

POSIX does not explicitly forbid concurrent iconv()/iconv_close() on the same descriptor, but a robust libc should not crash with UAF. Mitigation options:

1. Hold the handles() Mutex for the duration of iconv() (cheap, but serializes all conversions across descriptors).
2. Replace the HashSet<usize> registry with HashMap<usize, Arc<Mutex<IconvDescriptor>>> so iconv() takes an Arc clone before releasing the registry lock; iconv_close removes the registry entry and drops one Arc reference, but the active iconv() call's Arc keeps the descriptor alive until it returns.
3. Use a generational handle scheme (sequence counter + slot index) to detect stale handles.

Option 2 is recommended: per-descriptor mutex avoids serialization across descriptors, and Arc reference-counting cleanly handles the lifetime issue.

Severity: HIGH — UAF in interposed libc function under multi-threaded use.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-24T01:52:59.789930171Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-24T02:22:02.139294292Z","closed_at":"2026-04-24T02:22:02.137351905Z","close_reason":"fixed iconv descriptor lifetime race with Arc-backed registry","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"bd-ju7","title":"Drift: runtime_math module cadence classification (hot path vs cadence; strict vs hardened)","description":"Create an explicit classification of every runtime_math module (existing + new) along these axes:

- Hot-path vs cadence:
  - `hot`: runs inside `RuntimeMathKernel::decide` strict fast path
  - `cadence`: runs only on epoch boundaries / resample points
- Mode surface:
  - used in strict
  - used only in hardened
- Data dependencies:
  - uses only integer/fixed-point state
  - uses floats (must be cadence-only if strict budget matters)
  - uses locks/mutex (must not be on strict hot path)

## Why This Matters
This is the “extreme optimization” guardrail: we can’t reason about perf without knowing which modules execute when.

## Deliverable
- A table (in a bead comment or short doc) listing each module and its classification.
- The table must include legacy anchors: which API families/subsystems the module protects.

- No module remains “unknown”; every module is classified.

Full cadence classification of all 45 runtime_math modules.

## Architecture Pattern: Deferred-Update

```
decide() [hot path, every call]
├─ AtomicU8/U64 reads (43 modules) — lock-free, integer only
├─ Direct method calls (2 modules: risk, cohomology) — lock-free
└─ Every 64th call: resample_high_order_kernels()
    └─ risk.evaluate() [lock-free], oracle.lock() [Mutex]

observe_validation_result() [cold path, after validation]
├─ 43 Mutex-protected modules updated sequentially
│  └─ Each state encoded to AtomicU8 and stored
└─ 25-element severity vector fed to fusion
   └─ 18 meta-controllers updated in second cascade
```

## Hot-Path Modules (2, both lock-free):
1. **risk** — `upper_bound_ppm()` direct call, conformal arithmetic, u32
2. **cohomology** — `fault_count()` direct call, u64 counter

## Cadence-Only Modules (43, behind Mutex in observe):
All follow the pattern: Mutex<Controller> updated in observe → state encoded to AtomicU8 → read lock-free in decide.

### AtomicU8 scalar (37 modules):
spectral, rough_path, persistence, bridge, hji, mfg, padic, symplectic, sparse, topos, audit, changepoint, conformal, loss_minimizer, coupling, microlocal, serre, clifford, ktheory, covering, tstructure, atiyah_bott, pomdp, sos, admm, obstruction, operator_norm, malliavin, info_geometry, matrix_concentration, nerve_complex, wasserstein, kernel_mmd, pac_bayes, stein, lyapunov, rademacher, transfer_entropy, hodge, provenance, grobner, grothendieck

### AtomicU8[COUNT] family-indexed (3 modules):
eprocess, cvar, large_deviations

### AtomicU64 multi-field (3 modules):
design (ident_ppm, budget_ns, expected_ns, selected), fusion (bonus_ppm, entropy_milli, drift_ppm, dominant_signal), equivariant (alignment_ppm, orbit)

## Mode Gating: NONE
All 45 modules run in BOTH strict and hardened modes. Mode only affects action selection (Allow vs Repair), not controller activation.

## Barrier Oracle:
Called lock-free in decide() at line 1253 — `barrier.admissible()`. Read-only guard, no observation needed. Classified as **hot/read-only**.

## Design Subsystem:
Special case — Mutex acquired in decide() but ONLY every 256th call (line ~1157). Cadence-gated.

## Summary:
- 0 modules with forbidden ops on strict hot path (no exp/ln/matrix)
- All float computation deferred to observe phase behind Mutex
- decide() hot path is 100% integer/atomic reads + 2 lock-free calls
- Strict overhead dominated by bonus summation (~40 atomic loads)","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:18:31Z"}]}

+{"id":"bd-r25ks","title":"REVIEW: [HIGH] abort() does not unblock SIGABRT before raising — POSIX violation, suppresses core dump","description":"In crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/stdlib_abi.rs at lines 2297-2309, abort() raises SIGABRT via crate::signal_abi::raise without first unblocking SIGABRT.

POSIX 2008 mandates: 'If the SIGABRT signal is being held or ignored, abort() shall first unblock or unignore the SIGABRT signal.'

Current implementation:
1. fflush stdio
2. raise(SIGABRT)  -- if blocked, signal stays pending, no delivery, no termination
3. signal(SIGABRT, SIG_DFL)
4. raise(SIGABRT)  -- still blocked, still no delivery
5. sys_exit_group(134)  -- fallback, but no core dump

Consequences:
- Process exits with status 134, but no core file is generated (production debugging is impaired)
- POSIX-compliant programs that block SIGABRT around critical sections expect abort() to override the block; they will instead silently continue execution (because raise returns without delivery) -- but here we have sys_exit_group as fallback so termination is preserved
- The user's SIG_DFL re-raise behavior is bypassed when SIGABRT is blocked

Fix: Before the first raise(), call sigprocmask(SIG_UNBLOCK, ...) with a sigset containing only SIGABRT. Match glibc abort.c behavior: also clear SIG_IGN by resetting to SIG_DFL if currently SIG_IGN.

Severity: HIGH -- POSIX non-compliance + lost coredumps in production crashes.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-24T01:54:15.710736731Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-24T02:36:11.148658121Z","closed_at":"2026-04-24T02:36:11.146362986Z","close_reason":"abort() unblocks SIGABRT before raise; POSIX-compliant; commit 64fb99b2","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

-{"id":"bd-z4k96","title":"REVIEW: [HIGH] locale_abi.rs setlocale/newlocale/textdomain/bindtextdomain walk unbounded memory via CStr::from_ptr on user-controlled pointers","description":"Location: crates/frankenlibc-abi/src/locale_abi.rs

Four POSIX/GNU locale entrypoints read user-supplied string pointers through CStr::from_ptr with no length bound. A caller that passes a non-NUL-terminated pointer walks arbitrary process memory until an accidental zero byte — info-leak or segfault, reachable from the libc.so boundary.

Affected sites:
- setlocale line 125: `let name = unsafe { CStr::from_ptr(locale) }.to_bytes();`
- textdomain lines 351, 355: dereferences `*domainname` (line 351) then CStr::from_ptr (line 355) — both on user-supplied pointers.
- bindtextdomain lines 377, 391: CStr::from_ptr on both `domainname` and `dirname`.
- newlocale line 441: CStr::from_ptr on `locale`.

Other parts of this file (lines 662, 741, 750, 759) read known-terminated internal strings and are fine.

Reachability: setlocale is called by essentially every C/C++ program at startup; newlocale is used in POSIX.1-2008 thread-safe locale code; textdomain/bindtextdomain are used by every gettext-enabled program. An adversarial caller (or corrupted pointer) triggers an unbounded memory walk.

Severity: HIGH. The same pattern was just fixed in __assert_perror_fail (bd-9bijw); locale entry points are more commonly invoked, so this is at least as reachable.

Fix plan:
- Introduce a shared read_bounded_cstr helper that wraps scan_c_string + known_remaining and returns Option<Vec<u8>>.
- Replace each CStr::from_ptr in the four functions with read_bounded_cstr; on None, treat the locale/domain as invalid (null return / query-mode default) per each function's existing contract.
- Also null-check *domainname byte read in textdomain by reading through the helper instead of dereferencing directly.

Tests: setlocale/newlocale are not in the abort-barrier category — add a regression test that feeds a non-NUL-terminated buffer via a short page-aligned slice and asserts the return is null without walking past the allocation.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-24T00:21:23.751231844Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-24T00:21:27.488804539Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"bd-z7gt","title":"bd-3bg subtask: Iconv conformance+reproducibility gates with structured evidence","description":"Background:
- iconv completion claims require end-to-end evidence, not just generator/runtime unit tests.

Goal:
- Deliver conformance + reproducibility verification pack for iconv pipeline and runtime behavior.

Deliverables:
1) Conversion fixtures for included codecs.
2) Reproducibility gate artifacts for generated tables.
3) Drift checks tying docs/support matrix to implementation reality.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Conversion fixtures and reproducibility gates pass in CI.
- Evidence artifacts are complete and reviewable.

Verification & Logging:
- E2E fixture scripts with strict/hardened runs where relevant.
- Structured logs: trace_id, fixture_id, codec_pair, expected, actual, timing, artifact_refs.","acceptance_criteria":"## Acceptance Criteria

### Mandatory Verification Gate
1. Unit Test Evidence
- Add deterministic unit tests for nominal, boundary, and invalid-input paths in the touched modules.
- Include strict and hardened mode assertions when behavior differs by runtime mode.
- Include failure-mode tests covering repair/deny/default behavior with explicit expected outputs.

2. Integration + E2E Evidence
- Add or extend fixture-driven integration coverage in `tests/conformance` and/or `tests/integration` for this bead scope.
- Add a deterministic E2E script path (or harness command) with explicit setup/teardown and replayable seeds.
- Compare FrankenLibC outputs against host glibc fixtures (or documented replacement-level contract) for all touched symbols.

3. Logging + Traceability Evidence
- Emit structured logs (JSONL or deterministic text) that include bead ID, scenario ID, runtime mode, symbol family, decision path, and outcome.
- Verify logs expose repair/deny counters and reason codes so regressions are diagnosable from artifacts alone.
- Record artifact paths in bead notes (test logs, fixture diffs, benchmark outputs, conformance reports).

4. Performance + Regression Evidence
- Capture before/after baseline for latency/throughput on touched hot paths.
- Enforce no-regression threshold or document bounded tradeoff with quantitative rationale.

5. CI Gate Evidence
- `cargo fmt --check`
- `cargo check --all-targets`
- `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo test --all-targets`
- all bead-specific harness and E2E scripts pass with deterministic logs archived.","notes":"## Mandatory Test/E2E Logging Checklist
- [ ] Unit tests added/updated for happy-path, edge-case, and invalid-input behavior.

- [ ] Structured logs include: timestamp, bead ID, test scenario, runtime mode, symbol family, decision path, action (allow/repair/deny), and result.
- [ ] Failure logs include reason codes + minimal reproducible input payload metadata.
- [ ] Benchmark/perf logs include baseline, candidate, delta %, and pass/fail gate decision.
- [ ] CI artifacts include conformance report, E2E transcript, and summarized pass/fail matrix.
